planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained to moderately well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Mahua is a large tropical tree of paramount economic and cultural importance in India — the fleshy, sweet, edible flowers are used to distill a traditional fermented spirit (mahua liquor), the seeds yield a high-quality edible and industrial fat (mahua butter), and the tree is sacred to numerous Indian tribal communities.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, deep soil in full sun; mahua is drought-tolerant once established and thrives on poor soils. Madhuca longifolia (mahua or Indian butter tree) is a large, slow-growing tropical tree native to the forests of central and southern India, deeply woven into the culture and economy of indigenous tribal communities across Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, and Madhya Pradesh. The tree provides multiple distinct food, economic, and cultural products: the fleshy, sweet, cream-colored flowers (corollas) that drop prolifically in spring are edible and extremely sweet — they are eaten fresh (tasting like a mild honey-flower combination), sun-dried and used as a sweetener, fermented into mahua wine, and distilled into mahua liquor, one of India's most important traditional tribal spirits and an important livelihood product. The seeds contain 30–40% mahua butter (a semi-solid vegetable fat) with a composition similar to cocoa butter, used in traditional cuisine, soap-making, and cosmetics. The leaves provide fodder; the bark and flowers have Ayurvedic uses. Mahua is considered a 'tree of life' by many Indian adivasi (tribal) communities — the flowers are economically critical, often constituting a significant fraction of tribal household income in forest villages. A remarkable tree with enormous cultural depth and genuine multiple-use value.
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Madhuca longifolia
Tata Institute of Social Sciences — Mahua Economy and Tribal Communities

common_name: Indian Mulberry Root (Bai Ji Tian, Morinda Root)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in part shade; Morinda officinalis (Bai Ji Tian) is one of the 50 fundamental herbs in Traditional Chinese Medicine, used for over 2,000 years to tonify Kidney Yang, treat impotence, lower back pain, and bone weakness — the starchy, medicinal roots are harvested after 3–5 years of growth.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; harvest roots after 3–5 years. Morinda officinalis (bai ji tian or Indian mulberry root) is a trailing and climbing vine from southern China and Southeast Asia in the coffee family (Rubiaceae) — one of the 50 fundamental herbs in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) and a plant used continuously in Chinese medical practice for over 2,000 years. The medicinal part is the dried root (bai ji tian in Chinese, meaning 'a hundred joints stretching to the sky') — starchy, slightly sweet, mildly warming — used in TCM specifically to tonify (strengthen) Kidney Yang energy, treating impotence, infertility, lower back and knee pain and weakness, fatigue, and osteoporosis from a TCM perspective. It is one of the principal herbs in many male reproductive tonifying formulas (like the classic Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan). The root contains anthraquinones, polysaccharides, and flavonoids with studied effects on testosterone levels, bone density, and adaptogenic activity. As a garden plant in zone 8–12, it is an attractive, semi-woody vine with glossy, ovate leaves and small white flowers in rounded clusters, followed by compound, white, aggregate fruits similar to noni (M. citrifolia) but smaller. Quite different from commercial noni in use and chemistry despite being in the same genus.
sources: American Botanical Council — Bai Ji Tian
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; gambooge (Malabar tamarind) is a South Indian and Sri Lankan tropical tree producing pumpkin-ribbed, sour, yellow-orange fruits whose dried rind is an essential souring agent in Kerala and Konkani coastal cooking — and the source of hydroxycitric acid (HCA), the controversial supplement compound marketed for weight loss.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; plant multiple trees for cross-pollination as the species is polygamous. Garcinia cambogia (gambooge or Malabar tamarind) is a tropical fruit tree native to the Malabar Coast of India and Sri Lanka, producing small (2–3 inch), distinctively pumpkin-ribbed, yellow-orange, extremely sour fruits. The fresh fruit pulp is too acidic to eat raw, but the dried rind (kokum or kodampuli, depending on region) is an essential souring agent in Kerala, Konkani, and Sri Lankan coastal cooking — adding a fruity-tart, slightly sweet tartness to fish curries, sambars, and the famous Goan sol kadhi coconut drink. The dried rind is also used in Sri Lankan fish preparations and as a food preservative in traditional coastal cuisine. The fruit rind gained enormous international attention in the 1990s–2010s as a weight-loss supplement ingredient: it is the primary natural source of hydroxycitric acid (HCA), which was heavily marketed as a fat-burning compound — though clinical evidence for significant weight loss has been mixed. The tree is also the source of gamboge resin (from incisions in the bark), a yellow pigment historically used as a watercolor pigment. The tree needs multiple trees for fruiting as it is polygamous (different trees have different flower types).
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Garcinia cambogia
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Gambooge
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Garcinia

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Roxburgh fig is a large-leafed fig native to the foothills of the Himalayas and Southeast Asia, producing enormous, rounded, elephant-ear leaves 12–24 inches across and clusters of large, brownish-red, edible figs that form directly on the trunk and main branches (cauliflory), similar to jackfruit.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Roxburgh fig grows rapidly and produces large clusters of edible figs. Ficus auriculata (Roxburgh fig or timla fig) is one of the most ornamentally dramatic and useful fruiting figs in tropical horticulture — a large, fast-growing tree from the Himalayan foothills and Southeast Asia producing the largest leaves of any commonly cultivated Ficus: enormous (12–24 inch), rounded, dark-green, slightly hairy leaves with a bold, tropical texture that gives the plant a genuinely dramatic visual presence, resembling a cross between a giant philodendron and an elephant ear. The figs are equally remarkable: large (1.5–2.5 inch), distinctly ribbed, brownish-red when ripe, produced in pendant clusters directly on the trunk and older branches (cauliflory) — the same unusual fruiting pattern as jackfruit. The figs are sweet, moist, seedy, and pleasant to eat fresh when ripe, with a mild, sweet flavor; they are also eaten as a cooked vegetable when unripe in Indian and Southeast Asian cooking (stewed, pickled, or curried). In Himalayan and hill-station gardens in India, Roxburgh fig is grown as a major multi-use tree; in zone 9–12 Gulf Coast gardens, it is an outstanding bold-textured ornamental that also produces an edible crop.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Ficus auriculata
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Tropical Figs

common_name: Hardy Tapioca (Cold Hardy Cassava, Argentine Cassava)

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Hardy tapioca is an Argentine relative of cassava (M. esculenta) that is far more cold-tolerant than true cassava — surviving freezes to zone 8 and dying back to the ground but regrowing in spring — primarily grown in warm-temperate and subtropical gardens as an ornamental foliage plant for its deeply-cut, tropical-looking leaves.

directions: Plant rooted stem cuttings in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; dies back to roots in freezes but regrows. Manihot grahamii (hardy tapioca or Argentine cassava) is a cold-hardy Argentinian relative of true cassava (M. esculenta) — closely resembling its famous edible cousin in leaf shape (deeply palmately lobed, tropical-looking) and stem structure but significantly more cold-tolerant, surviving hard freezes to zone 8 (approximately 15°F) by dying to the ground and regenerating from tuberous roots the following spring. In zone 8–11, it is grown primarily as an ornamental for its dramatically tropical-looking, deeply cut, dark-green leaves (8–12 inches across) — creating a convincing tropical garden effect at far lower temperatures than true cassava (zone 10+). The plant can reach 4–8 feet in a single growing season, creating rapid impact as a tropical accent. The roots are reportedly edible (similar to cassava) but much less developed than commercial cassava and require the same caution about proper preparation to remove cyanogenic glycosides. Hardy tapioca is drought-tolerant, grows rapidly on poor soil, and is extremely low-maintenance — simply cut to the ground after frost and let it regrow. A remarkably useful plant for zone 8–9 gardeners seeking a tropical aesthetic without the winter protection demands of true tropical plants.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cold Hardy Tropicals
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ornamental Grasses and Tropical Plants
